proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the 77 binary features, several have univariate associations with `objective_response` at p<1e-5, with `feature_013` (a 65%-prevalent indicator) showing the largest negative association: response rate is lower in patients with feature_013=1 than in patients with feature_013=0.

Among the continuous and ordinal features, the ordinal `feature_051` (values 0/1/2) is the strongest predictor of `objective_response`: mean `feature_051` is lower in responders than in non-responders (i.e., response decreases monotonically with `feature_051`).
The binary `feature_006` (28% prevalent) is positively associated with `objective_response`: response rate is higher when feature_006=1 than when feature_006=0.

analyses.result_summary: Response rate 15.40% when feature_013=1 (n=32,365) vs 19.64% when feature_013=0 (n=17,635); chi-square p=1.11e-33. feature_013 is the strongest binary univariate predictor and is associated with reduced response.

Response rates by feature_051 are 21.08% (level 0, n=17,557), 15.70% (level 1, n=24,962), 11.05% (level 2, n=7,481). Logistic regression coefficient = -0.3749 per unit increase, OR=0.687, p=1.7e-93. Very strong monotonic negative association consistent with an ECOG-like performance-status variable.
Response rate 18.19% with feature_006=1 (n=14,258) vs 16.38% with feature_006=0 (n=35,742); chi-square p=1.04e-06. Modest positive effect.

proposed_hypotheses.text: `feature_011` (continuous, range 0-28) is negatively associated with `objective_response`: each unit increase in feature_011 lowers the odds of response.
`feature_067` (binary, 25% prevalent) is negatively associated with `objective_response`: patients with feature_067=1 have a lower response rate than patients with feature_067=0.
`feature_092` (continuous, range 0-0.8) has a small overall positive association with `objective_response`, with mean feature_092 higher in responders than in non-responders.

analyses.result_summary: Quintile response rates: 18.92%, 16.99%, 15.83%, 13.73% (top quintile). Logistic regression coefficient -0.0385 per unit, OR=0.962, p=1.8e-32. Strong negative monotonic association.
Response rate 14.36% when feature_067=1 (n=12,584) vs 17.75% when feature_067=0 (n=37,416); chi-square p=2.0e-18. Strong negative association.

analyses.code: from scipy.stats import ttest_ind; ttest_ind(df.loc[y==1,'feature_092'], df.loc[y==0,'feature_092'], equal_var=False)
analyses.result_summary: Mean feature_092: 0.3130 in responders vs 0.3056 in non-responders, t-test p=3.8e-3. Small positive overall association; tertile response rates 16.69%/16.51%/17.48% suggest most of the signal is at high feature_092.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Patient age (`feature_078`, range 30-90) is associated with `objective_response`, with response rates differing across age quintiles.
Self-reported race (`feature_123`) is associated with differential `objective_response` rates: white patients have a different response rate than non-white patients.

Insurance type (`feature_005`: medicare/medicaid/private/uninsured) is associated with `objective_response`, with private-insurance patients having higher response rates than medicaid/uninsured patients.

analyses.result_summary: Quintile response rates 16.99%/16.45%/17.51%/16.37%/17.15% are essentially flat. Logistic coefficient 0.00051 per year, OR per 10y=1.005, p=0.67. No meaningful age effect on response.

Response rates: white 16.55%, hispanic 17.15%, black 17.76%, asian 17.72%, other 18.54%. Chi-square p=0.045 (marginal). White patients have the lowest crude response rate; gap with black is +1.21 percentage points.
Response rates: medicaid 16.13%, medicare 16.85%, private 17.20%, uninsured 17.00%. Chi-square p=0.23. No statistically significant insurance disparity in crude response.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Histology (`feature_043`: adenocarcinoma vs squamous) modifies the association between `feature_006` and `objective_response`, with a larger positive feature_006 effect in adenocarcinoma than in squamous histology.

Smoking status (`feature_057`: current/former/never) modifies the association between `feature_007` and `objective_response`, with the positive feature_007 effect smaller in never-smokers than in current/former smokers.
Smoking status (`feature_057`) modifies the association between `feature_039` and `objective_response`, with the positive feature_039 effect smaller in never-smokers than in current/former smokers.

analyses.code: for hist, sub in df.groupby('feature_043'): ...chi2_contingency(pd.crosstab(sub['feature_006'], sub['objective_response']))
analyses.result_summary: feature_006 effect: adenocarcinoma rr1-rr0=+0.0189 (p=2.4e-5); squamous rr1-rr0=+0.0152 (p=0.026). Effect direction is the same in both histologies; difference is small.

analyses.code: for sm_, sub in df.groupby('feature_057'): rr1-rr0
analyses.result_summary: feature_007 effect: current rr1-rr0=+0.0139, former +0.0133, never +0.0008. Effect is essentially zero in never-smokers but positive in current/former smokers - a pattern consistent with a smoking-related response biomarker.

analyses.code: for sm_, sub in df.groupby('feature_057'): rr1-rr0
analyses.result_summary: feature_039 effect: current rr1-rr0=+0.0124, former +0.0108, never +0.0012. Similar pattern as feature_007 - benefit absent in never-smokers.

proposed_hypotheses.text: `feature_092` (continuous, 0-0.8) modifies the effect of `feature_006` on `objective_response`: the positive feature_006 effect is larger at higher feature_092 values than at lower values (positive interaction).
`feature_092` modifies the effect of `feature_007` on `objective_response`: the positive feature_007 effect is larger at higher feature_092 values (positive interaction).
`feature_092` modifies the effect of `feature_039` on `objective_response`: the positive feature_039 effect is larger at higher feature_092 values (positive interaction).

analyses.result_summary: Interaction coefficient feature_006:feature_092 = +0.594, p=1.18e-6. By feature_092 tertile, response rate when feature_006=1 vs 0: low PDL1 17.22% vs 16.48%; mid 17.20% vs 16.23%; high 20.10% vs 16.41%. Treatment-like benefit concentrated in high feature_092.
Interaction coefficient feature_007:feature_092 = +0.606, p=7.92e-8. High-tertile feature_092 shows 19.31% (feature_007=1) vs 15.98% (=0); low-tertile shows essentially no effect.
Interaction coefficient feature_039:feature_092 = +0.591, p=1.57e-7. High-tertile feature_092: 19.29% (feature_039=1) vs 15.68% (=0); low and mid tertiles show no effect or slight reverse.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Patients positive for all three putative-treatment indicators (feature_006=1 AND feature_007=1 AND feature_039=1) have a higher `objective_response` rate than patients with zero of the three positive.

The boost in `objective_response` from triple positivity (feature_006+feature_007+feature_039 = 3) is concentrated in the high-`feature_092` tertile, with a much smaller boost in the low/mid tertiles (three-way interaction).
`feature_013` and `feature_067` are independently negatively associated with `objective_response` after multivariable adjustment, with neither effect explained by the other.

analyses.result_summary: Response rate by sum: 0 of 3 = 16.12% (n=9,882), 1 of 3 = 16.42% (n=21,815), 2 of 3 = 16.79% (n=15,087), 3 of 3 = 22.98% (n=3,216). Triple positives have a +6.86 percentage-point boost over triple negatives, much more than additive.

Triple-positive response rate by feature_092 tertile: low 17.04%, mid 17.28%, high 34.25%. In low/mid tertiles, n_tx=3 is barely better than n_tx=0/1/2; in the high tertile, n_tx=3 doubles the response rate. Strong three-way interaction (treatments x feature_092).

In multivariable logistic regression with feature_051, feature_011, feature_092, feature_099, feature_063, feature_006/007/039, feature_013, feature_067, demographics: feature_013 coef=-0.302 (p=4e-34, OR=0.74); feature_067 coef=-0.259 (p=5e-19, OR=0.77). Both remain strongly negative and largely independent of each other and of other adjusted covariates.

proposed_hypotheses.text: After multivariable adjustment for clinical features (feature_051, feature_011, feature_092, treatments, feature_013, feature_067, age, smoking, histology, and insurance), `feature_123` (race) is no longer significantly associated with `objective_response`.
After multivariable adjustment, `feature_078` (age) remains non-associated with `objective_response`, with coefficient near zero.
`feature_099` (range 1.7-5.5) is independently positively associated with `objective_response` after multivariable adjustment, consistent with a serum-protein-like marker (e.g., albumin).
`feature_063` (range 0.03-296) is independently negatively associated with `objective_response` after multivariable adjustment, consistent with an inflammatory/marker analyte that worsens prognosis.

analyses.code: sm.Logit(y, X_full).fit() coefficients on feature_123 dummies
analyses.result_summary: Adjusted race coefficients (vs asian baseline): black +0.004 (p=0.94), hispanic -0.040 (p=0.49), other +0.048 (p=0.62), white -0.077 (p=0.13). None significant. The marginal univariate signal (white lowest crude RR) does not survive adjustment.
Adjusted age coefficient = 0.0003 per year (p=0.78). Age has no independent effect on response in this cohort.
Adjusted feature_099 coefficient = +0.093 per unit (p=1.2e-4). Independent positive effect; OR per +1 unit = 1.097.
Adjusted feature_063 coefficient = -0.0051 per unit (p=4.8e-4). Modest independent negative effect; OR per +10 units = 0.951.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The likelihood-ratio test for adding three feature_092 x treatment interactions (feature_092*feature_006, feature_092*feature_007, feature_092*feature_039) to the multivariable model is highly significant, supporting feature_092 as a predictive (effect-modifying) biomarker rather than a purely prognostic marker.

After adding the three feature_092 x treatment interactions, the main effects of feature_006, feature_007, and feature_039 become near-zero or negative, while the interaction terms are all positive and significant - implying treatment benefit is essentially absent at feature_092=0 and grows linearly with feature_092.

The joint best vs worst risk strata defined by clinical features (best: triple-positive, high feature_092, feature_051=0, feature_013=0, feature_067=0; worst: triple-negative, low feature_092, feature_051=2, feature_013=1, feature_067=1) differ in `objective_response` rate by at least 25 percentage points.

analyses.result_summary: LR test statistic = 79.29 on 3 d.f., p<1e-16. Adding the three feature_092 x treatment interactions improves model fit very substantially.

With interactions: feature_006 main = -0.059 (p=0.22), feature_007 main = -0.109 (p=0.012), feature_039 main = -0.110 (p=0.010); interaction coefficients +0.595/+0.609/+0.596 (all p<1e-6). At feature_092=0, treatments are neutral or slightly harmful; at feature_092=0.6, all three confer ~+0.30 log-odds (~OR 1.35) of response.
Best stratum (n=105): response rate 39.05%. Worst stratum (n=86): response rate 4.65%. Absolute gap = 34.4 percentage points; ratio ~8.4x.

proposed_hypotheses.text: `feature_013` (the largest negative binary predictor) is approximately uncorrelated with histology (feature_043), smoking (feature_057), race (feature_123), insurance (feature_005), age, and the labs we have measured - i.e., it is not explained by other observed covariates and represents an independent risk factor.

Smoking status modifies the effect of `feature_006`: the feature_006 benefit is at least as large in never-smokers as in current/former smokers (in contrast to features_007/039, where the benefit disappears in never-smokers).
ECOG-like `feature_051` does not strongly modify the effect of `feature_006`: the absolute response-rate benefit of feature_006=1 is similar across feature_051 levels 0/1/2.

analyses.code: df.groupby(...)['feature_013'].mean(); plus mean of continuous features by feature_013
analyses.result_summary: Prevalence of feature_013 is essentially constant across histology (0.645/0.653), smoking (0.65/0.65/0.64), race (range 0.64-0.66), and insurance (0.64-0.65). Mean continuous features differ by <0.01 between feature_013=1 vs 0. feature_013 is uncorrelated with measured covariates.

analyses.code: for sm_, sub in df.groupby('feature_057'): rr1-rr0 for feature_006
analyses.result_summary: feature_006 effect on response: current rr1-rr0=+0.0199, former +0.0154, never +0.0279. Benefit is largest in never-smokers - opposite pattern to features_007/039 (which were null in never-smokers).

analyses.code: for v, sub in df.groupby('feature_051'): rr1-rr0 for feature_006
analyses.result_summary: Absolute feature_006 benefit by feature_051: ECOG-like 0 = +0.0156, 1 = +0.0206, 2 = +0.0156. Benefit is roughly constant on the absolute scale, confirming feature_006 effect is not dramatically modified by performance status.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The dominant pattern in this NSCLC cohort is: response is driven by (a) ECOG-like performance status (feature_051, monotonically negative); (b) two unexplained risk factors feature_013 and feature_067 (both strongly negative); (c) a putative treatment-eligibility/exposure cluster (features 006/007/039) whose benefit depends almost entirely on a continuous biomarker (feature_092) that resembles PD-L1 expression; and (d) two prognostic labs (feature_099 positive, feature_063 negative).

Demographic variables - age (feature_078), race (feature_123), and insurance (feature_005) - are NOT significant predictors of `objective_response` after adjusting for clinical/biomarker features, with adjusted p-values >0.1 for race and >0.7 for age.

feature_007 and feature_039 are essentially independent of each other (chi-square p > 0.5) and of feature_006, suggesting random/independent assignment - i.e., these three features are likely separate exposures (e.g., different lines of therapy or three independent treatment indicators) rather than overlapping codes for the same exposure.

analyses.code: comprehensive multivariable Logit with interactions; summary of effect sizes
analyses.result_summary: Adjusted ORs (per unit): feature_051 OR=0.682; feature_013 OR=0.739; feature_067 OR=0.773; feature_011 OR=0.961/unit (×0.96^range = strong); feature_099 OR=1.097/unit; feature_063 OR=0.995/unit; feature_006 (at feature_092=0.6) OR ≈ 1.32; feature_007 OR ≈ 1.27; feature_039 OR ≈ 1.27. All p<0.01. Together explain the great majority of the response signal.

analyses.code: sm.Logit(y, X_full).fit() — adjusted coefficients on demographics
analyses.result_summary: Adjusted age coef 0.0003 (p=0.78). Adjusted race dummies (vs asian): white -0.077 (p=0.13), black +0.004 (p=0.94), hispanic -0.040 (p=0.49). Adjusted insurance: medicare +0.063 (p=0.10), private +0.089 (p=0.019), uninsured +0.083 (p=0.23). Only private vs medicaid is marginally significant. No evidence of demographic disparities in response after adjustment for clinical features.

analyses.code: from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency; for combinations of feature_006/007/039 pairs
analyses.result_summary: feature_006 x feature_007: chi2 p=0.31; feature_006 x feature_039: chi2 p=0.70; feature_007 x feature_039: each ~50% prevalence and unconditionally close to independent (~25% in each cell). Independence consistent with separate, independently-assigned exposures.
